excerpt: Islam comprises two essential dimensions: ʿibādāt (ritual devotion) and akhlāqiyyāt (moral conduct). The former reconnects us with our Creator; the latter binds us to His creation.
source_work: Rahat al-ʿAql / al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama
source_location_hint: On the dual nature of religious observance

excerpt: The concept of free will between predestination: 'We have guided you toward both paths'—the dual capacity for choice in the intermittent state between birth and death.
source_location_hint: On divine guidance and human volition

excerpt: The five foundations of Islam as enumerated in the hadith of the Prophet include shahāda, iqāmat al-salāt, itāʾ al-zakāt, ṣawm Ramadan, and ḥajj—the explicit pillars upon which the dīn rests.
source_location_hint: On the enumeration of Islamic obligations

excerpt: According to the Sulaymānī tradition, the seven pillars are: wilāya, ṭahāra, salāt, zakāt, ṣawm, ḥajj, and jihād—the implicit principles contained within the explicit five.
source_location_hint: On the esoteric completion of the pillars

excerpt: The verse of Ghadīr reveals perfection of dīn, completion of blessings, and Allah's contentment—a triple perfection achieved through the appointment of ʿAlī as successor on that sacred day.
source_location_hint: On the completion of the religion through the Imamate

excerpt: The declaration 'Man kuntu mawlāhu fa-hādhā ʿAlī mawlāhu'—the explicit transfer of mawlāyat from the Prophet to ʿAlī on the day of Ghadīr, establishing the principle of wilāya in the successive Imāms.
source_location_hint: On the succession and guardianship of ʿAlī

excerpt: The invocation following Ghadīr establishes that divine support and truth revolve with the appointed Imām, and that the Imāms descending from ʿAlī and Fāṭima are the embodiments of divine truth in successive ages.
source_location_hint: On the revolving of truth with the Imāms

excerpt: The divine address to humanity expressing mutual love—'I love you; therefore love Me in return'—establishes the reciprocal covenant between Creator and creation as the foundation of religious devotion.
source_location_hint: On divine love as the basis of the covenant
